paragraphs.body: Lipstick Stain
She’s standing by the window.

annotations.comment: I love the title. Here, you lost me a bit with the repetition. Could the story not begin, “She’s always standing by the window, looking pensive —“?

paragraphs.body: She always seems to be standing by the window, looking pensive — but, no, pensive isn’t quite the right word. Looking distant, looking frozen. She’s like a sculpture, perfectly still, watery sunlight pouring through the glass and silhouetting her in such a way that she seems to be cast from gold and imbued — haloed — with a honied glow. I’m brought to mind those mosquitos, the ones trapped and preserved in resin for a far longer time than anything should ever exist. A fossil trapped in time. A remnant of what it once was, lacking life.

annotations.text: through the glass and silhouetting her in such a way that she seems to be cast from gold and imbued — haloed — with a honied glow
annotations.comment: This is picky, second or third draft stuff, but I just want to note that you’ve spent six sentences on getting across, in various very nicely written ways, what she looks like.

paragraphs.body: And then there’s movement from her — slight, lest she startle and flee like a frightened deer. She lifts the mug to her lips and sips ever so slowly. For a moment, the mug hovers in front of her face, cradled in her hands like something dear and precious, something she can’t imagine ever letting go of, before she lowers it once more.

annotations.text: slight, lest she startle and flee
annotations.comment: I was a little confused by how her own movement could startle her?

paragraphs.body: Despite myself, I can’t help my gaze from straying to that mug, to the way her knuckles have turned white from gripping it a bit too strongly. Faint wisps of steam leap from the liquid’s surface, twining amongst each other weakly before dispersing, and I wonder just how long she’s been standing there, sipping from a mug of tea that’s growing cold. But, mostly, I look at the lipstick stain she leaves behind, the pink smudge on the white porcelain like a stroke of paint on a blank canvas. That’s how I think of her, how I always have — she’s an artist, her craft present in every beautiful thing she does, every graceful movement, every poetic sentence, every soft draw of breath.

annotations.comment: I was glad to see the story get underway a bit more. Intrigued by this relationship.

paragraphs.body: She hates that lipstick mark. I know she does.
So don’t put on lipstick, I suggested to her once.
The color is too pale without it, she’d said. Displeasure had creased her forehead and pinched her brow.

annotations.comment: Would this be “my color” or “my skin”?

paragraphs.body: So put it on after you finish the tea.
I don’t want to put it on.

And there it is. The paradox that has long puzzled me, its solution eluding me as surely as her smile. Yet I never push so far as to voice it. Three simple letters. One simple word. A question I will not give sound to. Perhaps because I know the answer well enough. An impossible answer, jagged and messy enough to tear one apart.

annotations.comment: I’m a little confused by the repetition here — it feels like you might be trying to be writerly, but while it’s nice writing, it’s slowing down the story. I’m about 25% of the way into it, and not much has happened.

paragraphs.body: It looks so fake, she would say if I asked. A thin guise for the thoughts screaming within her head. It’s fake. I’m fake.
I don’t want to hear her say it, so I tear my eyes away from that pink stain and instead ask if she’s eaten anything.
I’m not hungry.

annotations.comment: What’s the reasoning behind rendering the dialogue this way? It’s disorienting and I’m not sure it helps you.

paragraphs.body: Ah. Of course not. She never is, it seems.
So I turn away and attempt to pretend that I’m not disturbed by how frightfully thin she’s become or how frail she appears, as if a strong wind could blow her away, and then she’d be gone.

annotations.comment: So interested to know what this relationship is. Why does the narrator never use her name?

paragraphs.body: The thought turns my stomach.

I spare her another glance — it’s this wonderful quality of hers that there always seems to be a new detail to see, something novel, unexpected, worthy of noticing. She’s wearing a turtleneck sweater and blue jeans tucked into boots. A warm, cozy outfit, one might think, seeing her hands only half-visible and peeking out from the sleeves, the collar pulled high. But I know her — know the pains she takes in dressing, the intention and thought behind each article — and I can’t seem to dismiss the thought that she’s hiding within her clothes, covering as much skin as she can.

annotations.comment: Could you give me a sense of how these two are positioned? I keep imagining the narrator sort of staring at “her” but not sure if that’s right. What else is the narrator doing or supposed to be doing? If this is a mom, I have trouble believing she’s not doing something.
annotations.context_left: w her — know the pains she takes in dressing, the intention and thought behind each article — and I
annotations.context_right: ’t seem to dismiss the thought that she’s hiding within her clothes, covering as much skin as she ca

paragraphs.body: I bite my lip.
There was once when she burst through the front door after a long day of classes, looking flustered and distressed, her eyes wild and rife with panic. It lied, she told me breathlessly.
What? It had been the only thing I could think to say, seeing her like that.
The mirror. It lied.

annotations.comment: What a wild thing to say!

paragraphs.body: Without further explanation, she had rushed up the stairs and flung open the door to my bathroom. Alarmed, I’d followed her, only to find her standing in front of the mirror, hands braced on the sink, breathing hard. She stayed that way for several long minutes, staring at her own disheveled reflection, silently mouthing the thoughts raging within her, against her.
It's fake. I’m fake.

As abruptly as she’d come in, she turned on her heel and stormed out, slamming the door behind her. Receding into her own room, she tore off the outfit she’d so carefully picked that very morning and clawed at her scalp, pulling out the hair extensions she’d used for a bit of extra volume, an added thickness and shine. They’d looked great on her. She looked great. She had thought so only hours before. And I could do nothing but wonder what had changed.
It lied.

annotations.comment: I’m really struck by how frustrating it is that the narrator doesn’t engage with “her” about this moment. Why not?

paragraphs.body: I stood in the bathroom for the next hour, listening to her sobs through the wall, staring into the mirror without seeing anything at all.
It’s fake.
All of it fake. All of me fake.
It seems my mirror tells lies too.

annotations.text: It seems my mirror tells lies too.

paragraphs.body: I flick on the stove. Push a pad of butter around a pan until it melts. Crack an egg. A second. The sizzling, bubbling sound of the eggs frying dominates the silence stretching between us, and I hear it like a roaring in my ears. I rarely hear her speak much anymore, much less raise her voice or talk with any sort of passion or force.

annotations.comment: But the mother (?) is also not talking so this feels a little rich.

paragraphs.body: I lift the pan. Flip the eggs.

At this point, I’d settle for just a bit of animation from her — some interest in anything but the way she looks — instead of just watching her stand by the window, gazing out at an unchanging landscape.

annotations.comment: I absolutely love that the mom is cooking. But I’d also love for her to try to engage with the daughter.

paragraphs.body: And I hate the way she stands —  hunched with her slender, too-thin shoulders curling inward, like she’s attempting to collapse into herself, to disappear. She’s always resented her height, resented that she stands a head above most other girls her age, that she’s even got an inch or two on most guys. So she makes herself smaller, tucks her chin against her chest and tries to blend in with everyone else.
She hates being just one face in a crowd but can’t stand being different.

annotations.comment: It’s hard to square that this character knows her so well but also doesn’t engage with her.

paragraphs.body: I drop the eggs onto a plate and reach for the silverware drawer. At that moment, she puts her mug down and crosses her arms tightly over her chest, hugging herself.

And there it is 	— that feeling of something welling up within me, painful in its fullness, its sheer size and strength —  rising through my chest and clawing up my throat like a scream. I want to throw my arms around her, bundle her up and hold her close, tell her she’s safe. I want to tell her that she can stand tall and hold her chin high. I want to tell her that her sense of style is immaculate, that she looks good in everything she wears, that she’s not the only one who has ever used hair extensions. I want to tell her that her lips aren’t too pale and never have been. I want to tell her that she doesn’t need makeup, that I actually prefer it when she doesn’t wear any and I can see the purple smudges beneath her eyes that reveal just how hard she works, but that she can and should wear it if it makes her happy, makes her feel pretty. I want to tell her that she’s pretty — no, that she’s beautiful. I want to tell her that she’s talented and smart and that I’ll make her breakfast — anything she wants, anything at all — and brew her a new cup of tea so that she doesn’t have to drink that cold one.
She’s enough. She’s enough. She’s enough.

Not just enough — everything. She always has been. And I would crow it from the rooftop if it was what it took to reach her through that shell of doubt and fear and insecurity she’s shrouded herself with.
But I suspect it won’t. And it kills me that I don’t know what will, that I wonder if anything can.

I push the plate away, not caring that I’ve left the drawer wide open. I go to her. Stand beside her and wrap an arm around her shoulder — gently, careful not to place too much weight upon her, knowing she carries far too much already. She meets my eyes, and I hope she can see everything written there. Mostly, I hope she knows what I’ve told her a thousand times before. That I’m here. I’m here, beside her, and I’m not going anywhere. I’ll stay. I won’t leave her. I love her.
She has nothing to prove to me. To anyone.

And there it is — the faintest trace of a smile ghosting over her lips, wrinkling the skin around her eyes, lifting the shadows from her expression. It fades as quickly as it came, but it was there, and it was real, and it was enough.
She turns back to look out the window once more.

annotations.comment: There’s a lot to like in this delicate, thoughtful draft, Aria. I appreciated the sensitivity of what you wrote and smallness of it (I mean that in a good way). I have a couple of suggestions. For one, it’s very hard to follow as a reader why this mom (I think?) doesn’t try to engage with her daughter more. It becomes a story of watching one person watch another person, and even at five pages, that feels labored. I was really happy when some cooking started, but confused when the food didn’t turn out to be an attempt to engage the woman with the tea. In general, I just couldn’t follow why the narrator didn’t reach out (instead of just telling us how badly they wanted to). Now, you may feel that this is because they think they’ll be rebuffed. But it would be great to see that happen — the mom tries, the daughter cuts her off, etc. Yes, it’s probably happened before, but in my experience, people often keep trying, even in ways that they have to be aware will not work. And that would also help with my second suggestion, which is that it would be great to see the daughter (?) as a person outside of the mom’s rather gloomy, tortured view. Who is she? What makes her laugh? What is she thinking about? Does her mom annoy her? I understand that she’s trapped in a terrible disease/mindset but the mom is so determined to see her through that lens, and we never hear her say anything and have no access to her thoughts that she becomes a representative of a disease rather than a character. Maybe we only get a glimpse of who she really is (and what her relationship with her mom could be like) but even that glimpse would help. It’s very nice work, Kim. I’m eager to see what happens when you work on it.
